title: OPENJSON undetected by any pattern list
verification: Re-ran full pipeline: 38/38 OPENJSON procedures now have needs_review=True, classification=MANUAL_REDESIGN, routed to PySpark with explicit OPENJSON warning and original T-SQL preserved
evidence: 38 procedures use OPENJSON; output/databricks_sql/silver/webapi__insertcitiesfromjson.sql contained no executable SQL, needs_review=False
impact: Converted file silently does nothing at runtime; no signal anywhere flags the problem
mitigation: Added OPENJSON pattern to _COMPLEXITY_PATTERNS (sql_project_parser.py) and _DIALECT_PATTERNS (impact_analysis.py); added explicit OPENJSON procedural-factor detection in convert_procedure (sql_converter.py) forcing PySpark routing + needs_review=True

title: Empty-bodied conversions pass through as needs_review=False
verification: Verified via run_conversion.py: any LIFT_AND_SHIFT/PARTIAL_AUTOMATION procedure where _extract_core_dml finds zero statements now returns needs_review=True with an explicit MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED warning instead of a silent empty file
evidence: Same as F1 -- generalized: any object where _extract_core_dml finds zero statements still reported needs_review=False if no other risk pattern matched
mitigation: Added a safety-net branch in convert_procedure (sql_converter.py): if core_dml is empty, force needs_review=True and emit a warning stating the file is NOT a safe empty conversion, regardless of why extraction failed
affected_object_examples: Same 38 OPENJSON procedures (also protects against any future unrecognised construct, not just OPENJSON)

title: Temp-table regex \b#\w+\b never matches due to word-boundary bug
verification: re.search(r'#\w+', 'CREATE TABLE #CityChanges...') now matches; re-ran pipeline -- OLTP/DW:Sequences.ReseedSequenceBeyondTableValues now show '#\w+(+1)' in complexity_factors (previously empty)
evidence: Pattern never matched in either sql_project_parser.py or impact_analysis.py -- corrected pattern r'#\w+' matches; 10 real temp-table users found vs 0 detected
impact: Complexity/risk silently underscored for temp-table-only-risk objects
mitigation: Fixed both occurrences of the pattern (_COMPLEXITY_PATTERNS in sql_project_parser.py and _PROCEDURAL_PATTERNS in impact_analysis.py) from r'\b#\w+\b' to r'#\w+'

title: Shared mutable SSIS variable TableName conflicts with recommended parallelization
verification: Re-ran SSIS conversion: output/ssis_conversion_report.md now contains a 'Shared-Variable Scoping Hazards' table; correctly detected TableName (13 writers) and additionally caught TargetETLCutoffTime (2 writers, not explicitly named in the original finding)
evidence: User::TableName reassigned by 13 different 'Set TableName to X' tasks; target_state_architecture.md recommends parallel entity loads
impact: Race condition if naively parallelized; previously the per-entity job-parameter design avoided it by accident not by detection
mitigation: Added detect_shared_variable_hazards() to ssis_converter.py: scans SSIS_EXPRESSION tasks for @[User::X]= assignments, flags any variable written by more than one task, surfaced in ssis_conversion_report.md
affected_object_examples: User::TableName (13 writers); User::TargetETLCutoffTime (2 writers, newly discovered)

title: MERGE against table variable with compound += assignment has no Spark MERGE INTO equivalent
verification: Verified against the real fixture: convert_procedure now emits both the MERGE-against-non-persistent-target warning and the compound-assignment warning for DataLoadSimulation.PickStockForCustomerOrders, in addition to the pre-existing CURSOR/WHILE warnings
impact: Reviewer following the generic 'temp table' warning would attempt a literal MERGE INTO translation and hit unsupported syntax mid-implementation
mitigation: Added two new pattern checks in convert_procedure (sql_converter.py): MERGE-against-table-variable/temp-table detection, and compound-assignment-operator detection inside UPDATE SET, each with a specific rewrite recommendation

title: FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF incorrectly equated to Delta TIMESTAMP AS OF in design docs
verification: Re-ran impact analysis and SQL conversion; verified the corrected text appears in outputs/impact_analysis.md Section 8 and in the per-column warning on every converted temporal table; confirmed via object_complexity_scores.json that 9 previously-unflagged temporal tables (e.g. Application.DeliveryMethods) now have needs_review=True because the corrected warning text includes the MANUAL REVIEW REQUIRED marker
evidence: GetCityUpdates uses FOR SYSTEM_TIME AS OF @ValidFrom 9 times per-row inside a CURSOR loop; target_state_architecture.md and conversion_decisions.md recommended Delta Time Travel as the replacement
impact: Naive translation would have silently returned whole-table-snapshot rows instead of per-row historical validity windows -- a correctness bug invisible by inspection
mitigation: Retracted the Delta-Time-Travel equivalence claim in accelerator/analyzers/impact_analysis.py's Performance Risks section and accelerator/converters/sql_converter.py's system-versioned-column warning; both now state the correct target pattern (WHERE valid_from<=ts AND (valid_to>ts OR valid_to IS NULL)) and the warning now triggers needs_review on every temporal table, not just ones with another co-occurring risk
affected_object_examples: Integration.GetCityUpdates and the analogous Get*Updates procedure for every temporal-tracked entity; 9 previously-unflagged temporal tables now correctly flagged

title: Dynamic SQL would be invisible to static dependency graph
verification: No code change -- automation stance is CONTINUE because zero instances exist in this corpus; documented as a porting risk instead
evidence: Zero sp_executesql/dynamic EXEC() instances found in this corpus; dependency_graph.py extracts references via static regex only
impact: No active impact today; would silently produce an incomplete dependency graph and wrong topological order/blast radius if dynamic SQL existed
mitigation: Left as a documented forward-looking risk in adversarial_review.md rather than adding speculative code for a construct not present in any object today -- revisit if this accelerator is ported to a source repo that does use dynamic SQL
affected_object_examples: None in current corpus -- forward-looking risk

title: No cross-entity atomicity boundary on partial job failure
verification: Added Section 5 'Cross-entity consistency during partial failures' to deploy/rollback.md, including a concrete mitigation pattern (last-fully-consistent-run watermark tag) for consumers needing point-in-time consistency
evidence: 13 independent entity-load task chains; repair-run correctly resumes only the failed entity but leaves a point-in-time-inconsistent cross-entity snapshot visible to downstream consumers
impact: Downstream Gold-layer reports joining multiple entities could read a mixed-watermark snapshot during/after a partial failure window
mitigation: Documented as an inherited operational risk in deploy/rollback.md with a concrete mitigation pattern; this is an operational/architectural risk, not a code defect, so the fix is documentation plus a recommended pattern rather than a code change
affected_object_examples: All 13 entity load chains (City, Customer, Date, Employee, Movement, Order, Payment Method, Purchase, Sale, Stock Holding, Stock Item, Supplier, Transaction, Transaction Type)

title: parse_sqlproj silently drops duplicate-defined objects with no log
verification: Re-ran full pipeline -- no behavior change for this corpus (0 duplicate-defined objects, as expected), but verified the new code path via direct unit reasoning: duplicate definitions now attach a duplicate_definition_files list to the kept object instead of vanishing, and inventory_builder surfaces it as a manual_steps entry
evidence: seen_ids dedup in sql_project_parser.py silently discarded duplicate-id files with no logging; zero ALTER-only files exist in current corpus so this path was untriggered
impact: Future source repos that split CREATE/ALTER across files would silently lose schema changes with zero trace anywhere
mitigation: Changed parse_sqlproj's dedup logic to record dropped duplicate file paths on the kept object (duplicate_definition_files field) instead of discarding silently; inventory_builder._normalise_sql_object now adds a manual_steps entry whenever this field is present
affected_object_examples: None in current corpus -- guard is in place and tested against the no-duplicates case; would activate on first future occurrence

title: Medallion mapping naming convention has no collision guard
verification: Re-ran run_target_state_design.py: collision guard executed against all 112 mapped objects with zero collisions, completing successfully; guard code path verified to raise ValueError on collision via code review of the comparison logic
evidence: All 112 target_fqn values in medallion_mapping.csv verified unique today; DW objects drop schema prefix by design asymmetric vs OLTP objects which keep it
impact: No active collision; a future DW schema addition sharing a table name with an existing same-layer schema would have silently merged two source objects into one target table
mitigation: Added a hard uniqueness check at the end of build_medallion_mapping (target_state_design.py) that raises ValueError listing every colliding pair if any two source objects resolve to the same target_fqn
affected_object_examples: None in current corpus -- guard now active and verified to pass cleanly

title: Sequences mapped to BRONZE despite having no medallion data lifecycle
verification: Re-ran pipeline: inventory.json medallion_layer distribution now shows INFRASTRUCTURE: 38 (previously folded into BRONZE: 308); medallion_mapping.csv unaffected since SEQUENCE was already excluded from it by object-type filter
evidence: _assign_layer had no SEQUENCE entry and fell through to default BRONZE for all 34 SEQUENCE objects (38 once USER_DEFINED_TYPE objects are included); code-mapping table separately recommends GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY instead of porting the sequence object
impact: Implied an inflated Bronze migration scope in inventory-level reporting/counts
mitigation: Added a new INFRASTRUCTURE medallion_layer value in _assign_layer (inventory_builder.py) for SEQUENCE and USER_DEFINED_TYPE object types instead of defaulting them into BRONZE
affected_object_examples: Sequences schema objects across OLTP and DW (e.g. Sequences.CityID) plus USER_DEFINED_TYPE objects

title: WebApi/Website pass-through CRUD objects labeled Silver alongside true conformed Silver objects
verification: Re-ran target-state design: medallion_mapping.csv now has a layer_subcategory column; verified 26 objects tagged serving_passthrough and 8 tagged conformed (remaining Silver-eligible rows are non-OLTP or have no subcategory yet)
evidence: 74 WebApi/Website procedures and views mapped to SILVER; WebApi.Cities is a plain SELECT over Application.Cities with no joins or transformation vs Dimension.City's real SCD2 conformance also labeled SILVER
impact: Conflated two different categories of migration work under one layer label
mitigation: Added _layer_subcategory() in inventory_builder.py distinguishing 'serving_passthrough' (WebApi/Website schemas) from 'conformed' (true Silver objects); surfaced as a new layer_subcategory column in medallion_mapping.csv

title: PySpark-only conversion loses inline-SQL-callable shape for business-rule functions
verification: Verified against the real fixture: convert_function's PySpark stub for Website.CalculateCustomerPrice now includes a spark.udf.register() call and an explanatory comment, plus a tracked warning describing the registration
evidence: Website.CalculateCustomerPrice correctly routed to PySpark stub for its procedural complexity but original callers invoke it inline inside T-SQL SELECT statements; no spark.udf.register step was emitted
impact: Every caller site would have needed rewriting to either pre-compute and join or manually register as a UDF -- not mentioned anywhere in generated output
mitigation: Added an explicit spark.udf.register(...) block and explanatory comment to the PySpark function stub in convert_function (sql_converter.py) plus a new tracked warning describing why the registration preserves SQL-callability

title: ISNULL to COALESCE rewrite changes type-coercion semantics for mismatched argument types
verification: No code change -- automation stance is CONTINUE; no confirmed instance of actual harm found in this corpus after spot-checking
evidence: _SYNTAX_REWRITES mechanically rewrites ISNULL( to COALESCE( with no type check; ISNULL returns first-arg's type COALESCE returns highest-precedence type among all arguments
impact: No confirmed instance of actual harm in this corpus (mostly matched-type usages); would be silent for mismatched-type cases if they existed
mitigation: Left as a documented low-priority improvement in adversarial_review.md rather than adding a heuristic type-mismatch check for a risk with no confirmed real instance
affected_object_examples: No confirmed harmful instance found -- spot-checked clean

title: Generic TOP/LIMIT non-determinism warning emitted unconditionally regardless of actual TOP usage
verification: Verified via convert_view: the warning now only appears when TOP( is present without ORDER BY in the specific view body, and now contributes to needs_review when it fires (previously it was header-only boilerplate that never affected the disposition)
evidence: Warning text was identical and always present on every converted view, whether or not TOP was used or an ORDER BY already existed
impact: Trained reviewers to ignore the warning as boilerplate noise rather than a genuine signal
mitigation: Made the warning conditional on actual TOP-without-ORDER-BY detection in convert_view (sql_converter.py); it is now a tracked warning contributing to needs_review rather than static header text
affected_object_examples: All converted views -- warning now fires only when genuinely applicable
